subtopic_detail: Metabolic monitoring and intervention on olanzapine
stem: You are the community psychiatry registrar reviewing a 38-year-old man with schizophrenia who has been stable on olanzapine 15 mg nocte for 2 years following several relapses on lower doses. A switch to aripiprazole 8 months ago caused an acute relapse requiring admission. His family report substantial weight gain over the past 18 months. He acknowledges a sedentary lifestyle and increased calorie intake but reports stable mood with no psychotic symptoms.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/86, HR 78, weight 95 kg (was 78 kg at olanzapine start), BMI 31
data_table.Examination: Central adiposity; no extrapyramidal signs; mental state stable
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 6.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 6.0 percent, total cholesterol 5.8, LDL 3.6, HDL 0.9, triglycerides 2.6 mmol/L; UEC and LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Refer for a structured lifestyle programme alone and repeat fasting glucose and lipids in 6 months
options.rationale: Lifestyle alone is insufficient when impaired fasting glucose and dyslipidaemia are already present; metformin augmentation is supported in AU practice for antipsychotic-related metabolic syndrome. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring)

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg twice daily with structured lifestyle changes while continuing olanzapine
options.rationale: Adjunct metformin reduces weight by 2-5 kg and improves glycaemic markers in patients with antipsychotic-associated metabolic syndrome without destabilising mental state, when continued antipsychotic efficacy is required. (Source: RANZCP; AMH)

options.text: Switch olanzapine to aripiprazole and provide lifestyle counselling at the same psychiatric review
options.rationale: The previous aripiprazole trial caused acute relapse with readmission; further switching risks decompensation in a patient with established efficacy on olanzapine. (Source: RANZCP)

options.text: Reduce olanzapine to 7.5 mg nocte and add a glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist
options.rationale: Dose reduction risks psychotic relapse; GLP-1 RAs are not first-line in Australia for antipsychotic-associated weight gain and are unfunded for this indication. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Refer for bariatric surgical assessment as the next stage of weight management
options.rationale: Bariatric review is appropriate only after structured medical and pharmacological weight management has been trialled and failed. (Source: NHMRC Obesity Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Metabolic syndrome affects 30-50 percent of patients on second-generation antipsychotics, with olanzapine carrying the highest risk. Australian monitoring schedules recommend weight, waist, BP, fasting glucose and lipids at baseline, 3 months, then at least annually. Metformin is the first-line pharmacological adjunct for weight gain and dysglycaemia in patients who require continued antipsychotic therapy. Switching antipsychotics is preferred when psychiatrically safe, but past relapse on aripiprazole here makes maintaining olanzapine plus metformin the safer combination.
key_points: Olanzapine has the highest metabolic risk among atypicals
Annual fasting glucose, lipids, weight, waist for all patients on antipsychotics
Metformin first-line adjunct with 2-5 kg weight reduction
Past relapse on switching argues against repeating that strategy
explanation.pearls: Metformin 500 mg twice daily started early in olanzapine treatment can prevent around half the expected weight gain at 12 months in first-episode psychosis, but onset of benefit is gradual and takes 8-12 weeks to plateau.

subtopic_detail: Adult ADHD diagnostic pathway with comorbid substance use
stem: A 28-year-old man presents to your GP clinic asking for assessment of adult ADHD. He has been struggling with chronic disorganisation and missed deadlines and was recently dismissed from a third role in 5 years for performance issues. He uses cannabis daily to 'calm down' and has been using since age 17. Mood is mildly low secondary to job loss but not pervasively depressed. He brings online ASRS-v1.1 self-screening results scoring 5/6 in Part A. He has not been previously assessed and brings no childhood records.

data_table.Examination: Restless, fidgety, tangential at times but conversation coherent; no psychomotor retardation; no thought disorder

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange childhood collateral and refer to a psychiatrist for structured ADHD assessment alongside cannabis cessation support
options.rationale: Adult ADHD diagnosis in Australia requires evidence of symptoms before age 12, multi-setting impairment, and exclusion of mimics such as substance use and mood disorders; cannabis stabilisation is needed because chronic use confounds diagnostic clarity. Only psychiatrists, paediatricians or neurologists can initiate stimulants under PBS Authority. (Source: RANZCP ADHD professional practice guideline; PBS)

options.text: Commence a 4-week trial of methylphenidate immediate-release and review response as diagnostic confirmation
options.rationale: Stimulant trials in active cannabis use risk masking the substance use disorder and are not diagnostic confirmation; stimulants must follow an established diagnosis. (Source: RANZCP)

options.text: Order an electroencephalogram and a structural brain MRI before any further specialist referral today
options.rationale: EEG and MRI are not part of standard adult ADHD assessment; they are reserved for focal neurological signs or atypical features. (Source: RACGP)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily as the first-line treatment of adult attentional symptoms in this patient
options.rationale: Sertraline is not indicated for primary attentional symptoms; first-line ADHD pharmacotherapy is stimulants once diagnosis is established. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Refer for neuropsychological testing as the single gold-standard diagnostic before any further action today
options.rationale: Neuropsychological testing supports but is not diagnostic alone; childhood history and collateral remain essential. (Source: RANZCP)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis in Australia requires DSM-5 criteria including childhood onset before age 12, pervasive multi-setting impairment, and exclusion of better explanations. Substance use, mood, anxiety, and sleep disorders mimic and co-occur with ADHD; stabilising these first improves diagnostic accuracy. Only specific specialists (psychiatrists, paediatricians, neurologists) can initiate stimulant pharmacotherapy under PBS Authority. Stimulant choice and titration consider cardiovascular risk, substance use risk, and adherence patterns.
key_points: DSM-5: childhood-onset before age 12, multi-setting impairment
Collateral history (childhood) essential; ASRS alone is screening not diagnosis
Cannabis use disorder must be stabilised before stimulant trial
PBS Authority required: psychiatrists, paediatricians, neurologists only
explanation.pearls: ASRS-v1.1 Part A with four or more positive items has sensitivity around 68 percent for adult ADHD but specificity only around 80 percent, so a positive screen always requires formal diagnostic interview and childhood collateral.

subtopic_detail: Smoking cessation and CYP1A2 interaction with clozapine
stem:
You are the psychiatric registrar in a community mental health clinic reviewing a 52-year-old man with schizophrenia who has been stable on clozapine 450 mg daily for 6 years. He smokes 25 cigarettes daily and now wants to quit after a recent CT chest showed early emphysematous changes. His daughter is having a baby and he wants to be smoke-free.

He has no current depression and no cardiovascular disease. His last clozapine level 4 weeks ago was 540 ng/mL (target 350-600). He plans to stop completely within 2 weeks.

data_table.Examination: Mental state stable; mild bilateral basal crackles
data_table.Investigations: Clozapine level 540 ng/mL (4 weeks ago); FBC WCC 6.2 with neutrophils 3.4; LFTs normal; ECG sinus rhythm with QTc 430 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence varenicline 0.5 mg titration as monotherapy and maintain the current clozapine dose throughout the cessation period
options.rationale: Varenicline is effective, but clozapine levels rise 50-70 percent on smoking cessation due to loss of CYP1A2 induction; unchanged dosing risks toxicity (seizures, sedation, myocarditis). (Source: AMH; eTG Psychotropic)

options.text: Commence nicotine replacement therapy at full dose and maintain the current clozapine dose throughout the cessation period
options.rationale: NRT is appropriate, but the metabolic issue is the same: stopping smoking removes CYP1A2 induction and clozapine levels rise; dose adjustment is required. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Commence varenicline 0.5 mg titration and reduce clozapine dose with serial clozapine-level monitoring during cessation
options.rationale: Smoking cessation removes CYP1A2 induction; clozapine clearance falls and levels rise 50-70 percent. Stepwise dose reduction (typically 25-30 percent over 1-2 weeks) with serial level monitoring prevents toxicity while supporting cessation. Varenicline has the highest abstinence rate at 12 months in AU studies. (Source: AMH; eTG Psychotropic; RACGP Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Defer cessation pharmacotherapy and recommend behavioural therapy alone with monthly review until he stops independently
options.rationale: Withholding effective pharmacotherapy from a motivated patient is not standard care; behavioural therapy alone has lower quit rates. (Source: RACGP)

options.text: Switch clozapine to olanzapine before commencing any cessation pharmacotherapy to avoid medication interactions today
options.rationale: Switching from an effective clozapine regimen risks relapse; clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant illness and switching is rarely justified for smoking-cessation alone. (Source: RANZCP)

explanation.summary: Tobacco smoke is a potent CYP1A2 inducer, accelerating clozapine clearance. On cessation, clozapine plasma concentrations rise by 50-70 percent within 2-4 weeks, risking sedation, hypotension, seizures, ileus and myocarditis. Australian practice is to anticipate clozapine dose reduction during cessation (typically 25-30 percent over 1-2 weeks), with clozapine-level monitoring at baseline, 1 week, 2 weeks, then monthly. Varenicline is first-line cessation pharmacotherapy with the highest quit rates; older neuropsychiatric concerns have been largely refuted by recent RCTs and post-marketing data.
key_points: CYP1A2 induction by smoking - clozapine clearance drops on cessation
Plasma clozapine rises 50-70 percent within 2-4 weeks of stopping
Anticipate 25-30 percent clozapine dose reduction
Varenicline first-line; NRT second-line; bupropion third (lowers seizure threshold)
explanation.pearls: Caffeine intake also affects clozapine via CYP1A2 - patients reducing or stopping caffeine simultaneously can compound the rise in clozapine levels; monitor caffeine intake at the cessation review.

subtopic_detail: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose with sodium-channel blockade
stem: A 24-year-old woman is brought to your emergency department by ambulance after being found unconscious by her flatmate next to an empty box of amitriptyline tablets prescribed for chronic neuropathic pain. Time of ingestion is unknown but the flatmate last saw her 6 hours ago. She is intubated by anaesthetics for airway protection. You are leading her resuscitation in resus.

data_table.Vital signs: GCS 7 prior to intubation; BP 88/52; HR 132; T 36.8; SpO2 99 percent on FiO2 50 percent post-intubation
data_table.Examination: Dilated pupils; warm dry skin; absent bowel sounds
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia 132, QRS 140 ms, terminal R wave aVR 5 mm, QTc 480 ms; VBG pH 7.28, lactate 3.4, bicarbonate 18; paracetamol and alcohol levels pending

lead_in: After the airway is secured, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous physostigmine 0.5 mg over 5 minutes to reverse anticholinergic toxicity
options.rationale: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose because it precipitates asystole and seizures in the setting of sodium-channel blockade. (Source: AMH; eTG Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intravenous lipid emulsion 20 percent 1.5 mL/kg bolus followed by infusion now
options.rationale: Lipid emulsion is a salvage therapy reserved for refractory cardiac arrest or shock unresponsive to bicarbonate and standard measures, not first-line. (Source: NSW Health Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil 200 micrograms repeated to a total of 1 mg to reverse coma
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated here; it has no role in TCA toxicity and risks seizure precipitation. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg bolus and recheck QRS width after each dose
options.rationale: QRS prolongation greater than 100 ms in TCA overdose indicates fast sodium-channel blockade; IV sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg corrects acidosis and provides sodium load, narrowing the QRS and stabilising cardiac sodium channels. Repeat boluses titrated to QRS narrowing and serum pH 7.45-7.55. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10 minutes to shorten the QTc interval
options.rationale: Magnesium is reserved for torsades de pointes; the threat here is broad-complex tachycardia from sodium-channel blockade, not QT-related torsades. (Source: ANZCOR)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes mixed toxicity: sodium-channel blockade (QRS widening, terminal R wave in aVR), anticholinergic effects (delirium, tachycardia, mydriasis, ileus), and seizures. QRS greater than 100 ms predicts seizures (15-30 percent risk) and greater than 160 ms predicts arrhythmia (50 percent risk). The cardiotoxic mechanism is shared with class IA antiarrhythmics; sodium bicarbonate alkalinises serum, increases extracellular sodium, and reduces drug-channel affinity, narrowing the QRS within minutes. Target pH 7.45-7.55. Activated charcoal is only useful within 1-2 hours of ingestion with a secured airway.
key_points: QRS over 100 ms = seizure risk; over 160 ms = arrhythmia risk
Terminal R wave aVR greater than 3 mm is specific for sodium-channel blockade
IV sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg, titrate to QRS and pH 7.45-7.55
Avoid physostigmine, flumazenil, class IA/III antiarrhythmics
explanation.pearls: Amitriptyline ingestion of 1 g (50 x 20 mg tablets) is potentially lethal in an adult of average weight; patients deteriorate rapidly within 2-6 hours of ingestion, so ED observation periods less than 6 hours are unsafe.

stem: A 32-year-old man with no past psychiatric history is admitted from the emergency department to your inpatient psychiatry unit with progressive social withdrawal, mutism, and immobility for 5 days. His wife reports he stopped eating and drinking 2 days ago. On examination he sits motionless, will not respond to questions, and his arm remains in the position you place it. He postures with an awkward arm held against gravity. Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale scores 17. He is intermittently staring and grimacing.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8, BP 102/68, HR 92, RR 18, SpO2 98 percent RA, weight 72 kg
data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile, waxy flexibility, posturing, grimacing; no rigidity, no tremor, mild fever only
data_table.Investigations: Na 140, K 4.0, creatinine 78, CK 410, FBC normal, urinary toxicology negative, TFTs normal, CT brain non-contrast normal
data_table.Medications: Nil regular; trial of risperidone 2 mg given 24 hours ago in ED

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous lorazepam 1-2 mg as a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge with response assessed at 15 minutes
options.rationale: A lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV with response at 15 minutes) is both diagnostic and therapeutic for catatonia; response rate is 70-80 percent. If partial, repeat doses or scheduled lorazepam 6-hourly are titrated. (Source: RANZCP; eTG Psychotropic)

options.text: Withhold all antipsychotics and arrange urgent electroconvulsive therapy within the next 12 hours
options.rationale: ECT is the next escalation if benzodiazepine challenge fails or in life-threatening malignant catatonia; a trial of benzodiazepines comes first when not malignant. (Source: RANZCP ECT guideline)

options.text: Continue risperidone and add olanzapine 10 mg to consolidate antipsychotic coverage during catatonia
options.rationale: Antipsychotics in untreated catatonia risk precipitating neuroleptic malignant syndrome; further antipsychotic load is contraindicated. (Source: eTG Psychotropic)

options.text: Administer intravenous haloperidol 5 mg with promethazine 25 mg as rapid sedation per the unit protocol
options.rationale: Adds further antipsychotic load and is contraindicated; haloperidol is associated with NMS in catatonia. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Administer intravenous dantrolene 1 mg/kg for presumed neuroleptic malignant syndrome from the risperidone dose
options.rationale: Dantrolene is reserved for confirmed NMS; this patient's CK is mildly elevated but vitals and rigidity do not meet NMS criteria; catatonia and NMS may overlap but treatment of underlying catatonia comes first. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a syndrome of psychomotor disturbance most commonly arising from mood, psychotic, or general medical conditions. Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale of 3 or more of 14 screening items supports diagnosis; 14 or more total signals severe catatonia. Treatment is lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV, repeat to 6-hourly), with ECT for non-responders or malignant catatonia. Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and may precipitate NMS; they are withheld until catatonia resolves and the underlying psychosis is treated separately.
key_points: Bush-Francis 3 or more of 14 screening = supports catatonia
Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV challenge first-line; 70-80 percent response
ECT if benzodiazepine fails or malignant catatonia
Avoid antipsychotics until catatonia resolved
explanation.pearls: A negative lorazepam challenge does not rule out catatonia; the trial dose is too low for some patients, and scheduled lorazepam 1-2 mg every 4-6 hours often produces delayed response within 48 hours - escalate to ECT only after this trial.

subtopic_detail: Autoimmune encephalitis presenting as first-episode psychosis
stem:
A 22-year-old previously well woman is admitted to your inpatient psychiatric unit with new-onset psychosis, prominent paranoid delusions, agitation, and disorganised speech progressing over 10 days. Family report she became uncharacteristically forgetful 3 weeks ago. On day 3 of admission she develops orofacial dyskinesias, intermittent rigidity, autonomic lability (BP 175/100 then 90/55 within 30 minutes), low-grade fever 38.2, and one witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure.

She was started on olanzapine 10 mg on admission. Her family deny any drug or alcohol use, and there is no personal or family history of psychiatric illness.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2, BP swinging 175/100 to 90/55, HR 60 to 130, RR 22, SpO2 96 percent RA
data_table.Examination: Orofacial dyskinesias, intermittent rigidity, fluctuating consciousness, brisk reflexes globally
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFT normal; CK 1100; urine toxicology negative; CT brain non-contrast normal; ECG normal; pregnancy test negative; troponin normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Cease olanzapine and commence haloperidol with scheduled lorazepam to manage agitation, posturing and autonomic features today
options.rationale: Symptoms beyond psychosis (seizures, dyskinesias, autonomic instability) are not consistent with primary psychiatric illness; antipsychotic switching without organic workup misses an organic encephalitis. (Source: RANZCP)

options.text: Transfer to intensive care and treat presumptively for neuroleptic malignant syndrome with dantrolene and supportive measures
options.rationale: NMS is plausible (rigidity, fever, CK) but the syndrome predates antipsychotic exposure and includes seizures, suggesting autoimmune encephalitis as primary, with NMS as a possible secondary diagnosis. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Continue olanzapine, add propranolol for autonomic symptoms and a midazolam infusion to manage seizures during stabilisation
options.rationale: Symptomatic management without identifying the underlying cause delays disease-modifying therapy; outcomes worsen significantly with each week of treatment delay in anti-NMDAR encephalitis. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Cease olanzapine and arrange urgent electroconvulsive therapy for presumed antipsychotic-resistant first-episode psychosis
options.rationale: ECT is reserved for treatment-resistant psychosis or catatonia after organic causes are excluded; in suspected autoimmune encephalitis it does not address the underlying disease. (Source: RANZCP)

options.text: Cease olanzapine, arrange urgent CSF autoantibody testing, commence intravenous methylprednisolone and immunoglobulin, and organise pelvic imaging
options.rationale: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is the most common autoimmune encephalitis in young women, with psychiatric prodrome followed by seizures, dyskinesias, autonomic instability, and reduced consciousness. CSF antibody testing confirms diagnosis; first-line treatment is IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days plus IV immunoglobulin or plasma exchange; ovarian teratoma (in 30-40 percent of women) is detected on pelvic imaging and removal accelerates recovery. (Source: BMJ Best Practice; ASCIA)

explanation.summary: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is the most common autoimmune encephalitis. The classic course is a prodromal viral-like illness followed by prominent psychiatric symptoms (mistaken for first-episode psychosis), then movement disorders, autonomic instability, seizures, and reduced consciousness. Diagnosis is by CSF IgG to NMDA-receptor antibody; serum antibody alone has lower sensitivity. First-line immunotherapy is IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days plus IV immunoglobulin or plasma exchange; second-line includes rituximab and cyclophosphamide. Ovarian teratoma is found in 30-40 percent of female patients; removal accelerates recovery. Delayed treatment correlates with worse long-term cognitive outcomes.
key_points: Psychiatric prodrome + movement disorder + seizures + autonomic instability = autoimmune encephalitis until proven otherwise
CSF anti-NMDAR antibody more sensitive than serum
First-line: high-dose IV methylpred plus IVIG or plasma exchange
Tumour search mandatory (ovarian teratoma in 30-40 percent of women)
explanation.pearls: Symptom-onset to first-line immunotherapy within 4 weeks is the single strongest predictor of full neurological recovery in anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis; delays beyond 4 weeks are associated with persistent cognitive deficit in 30-40 percent of patients.

stem: A 68-year-old man is brought to your emergency department from his nursing home with 24 hours of fevers, confusion and reduced oral intake. He has type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and previously had a transurethral prostatectomy. On arrival he is hypotensive, tachycardic, and confused. He is unable to provide a coherent history. The triage nurse has placed two large-bore peripheral cannulas. You are the ED registrar leading the resuscitation.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9, BP 88/52, HR 118, RR 26, SpO2 92 percent RA, GCS 13 (E3V4M6)
data_table.Examination: Confused, mottled lower limbs, mild suprapubic tenderness; chest clear; warm peripheries
data_table.Investigations: Bedside lactate 4.2 mmol/L; VBG pH 7.30, bicarbonate 17; finger-prick glucose 11.4 mmol/L

lead_in: Within the first hour, what is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Obtain blood cultures, administer 30 mL/kg crystalloid, and commence empiric intravenous antibiotics only once culture results return
options.rationale: Blood cultures should be taken before antibiotics, but antibiotics must not wait for results; in septic shock each hour of delay increases mortality 7-8 percent. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign; ANZICS)

options.text: Obtain blood and urine cultures, give empiric intravenous antibiotics within an hour, administer 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid, and recheck lactate at 2 hours
options.rationale: The hour-1 bundle for septic shock includes lactate measurement, blood and other cultures before antibiotics (without delaying antibiotics), empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate 4 or higher, and lactate re-check. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign; eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence peripheral noradrenaline infusion without initial fluid resuscitation given the hypotension and rising lactate today
options.rationale: Vasopressors are second-line after fluid resuscitation; peripheral noradrenaline is acceptable when refractory but not as initial intervention. (Source: Surviving Sepsis)

options.text: Administer a 1 L crystalloid fluid challenge and reassess the need for intravenous antibiotics only after blood pressure normalises
options.rationale: A 1 L challenge is inadequate; ANZICS and Surviving Sepsis recommend 30 mL/kg within 3 hours. Antibiotics must not be deferred until BP normalises. (Source: ANZICS)

options.text: Insert a urinary catheter for source control, perform CT abdomen pelvis, and commence antibiotics only after imaging confirms the source
options.rationale: Source control imaging is important but must not delay early antibiotics; bedside catheterisation can occur alongside but does not replace empiric therapy. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Sepsis with septic shock requires the Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle: measure lactate (remeasure if elevated), obtain blood cultures before antibiotics (do not delay antibiotics), administer broad-spectrum empiric IV antibiotics, give 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate 4 or higher, and start vasopressors if MAP stays below 65 after fluid. Likely urinary source here (nursing home, suprapubic tenderness, prior prostatic surgery) justifies empirically piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone plus gentamicin in line with eTG. Antibiotic timing is the single largest modifiable mortality factor.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors if needed
Each hour of antibiotic delay raises septic shock mortality 7-8 percent
30 mL/kg crystalloid not 1 L
Cultures before antibiotics but never delay antibiotics
explanation.pearls: Lactate over 4 mmol/L on presentation has an associated in-hospital mortality around 27 percent for sepsis even when blood pressure normalises after fluid; serial lactate trend at 2 hours is a stronger prognostic indicator than the initial value.

subtopic_detail: Empiric antibiotic regimen in IVDU native valve IE
stem: A 38-year-old man presents to your emergency department with 2 weeks of fevers, night sweats, and weight loss. He uses intravenous heroin and admits to recent injecting through the femoral vein. Examination shows a new pansystolic murmur loudest at the lower left sternal edge, splinter haemorrhages on two fingernails, and bilateral basal crackles. You suspect infective endocarditis and are awaiting an urgent transthoracic echocardiogram. Three sets of blood cultures from separate sites have been drawn.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6, BP 110/72, HR 108, RR 22, SpO2 94 percent RA
data_table.Examination: New PSM 3/6 at LLSE, splinter haemorrhages, JVP elevated 5 cm, bibasal crackles
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.4 (neutrophils 14.2), CRP 220, Hb 102, platelets 145, creatinine 95; ECG sinus tachycardia; CXR pulmonary congestion with small bilateral effusions; troponin 24 (mildly raised); blood cultures pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin alone pending culture results and consideration of surgical management
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin alone is inadequate for an intravenous drug user where S. aureus is most likely (over 60 percent of right-sided IE); broader gram-positive coverage including flucloxacillin or vancomycin is required. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate while awaiting echocardiogram and definitive blood culture results
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are not appropriate for empiric IE coverage; intravenous is mandatory. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin plus intravenous gentamicin pending culture results and echocardiogram
options.rationale: For native valve IE in an intravenous drug user, eTG recommends empiric IV flucloxacillin 2 g 4-hourly plus gentamicin (single daily dosing per renal function) to cover S. aureus and streptococcal species pending culture results; vancomycin substitutes if penicillin-allergic or MRSA likely. (Source: eTG Antibiotic; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone alone with no additional empirical gram-positive coverage at this stage
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone alone misses MSSA at usual doses and does not provide adequate cover for the most likely organism. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin alone with no additional empirical gram-negative coverage at this stage
options.rationale: Vancomycin monotherapy is reserved for confirmed MRSA or penicillin allergy; gram-negative cover via gentamicin enhances activity against streptococci and provides synergy. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Suspected infective endocarditis warrants empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics after three sets of blood cultures from separate venepunctures. In a native valve IE with intravenous drug use, S. aureus is the dominant pathogen; empiric flucloxacillin plus gentamicin is first-line per Australian guidelines. Vancomycin replaces flucloxacillin if MRSA suspected (recent hospitalisation, known colonisation, severe penicillin allergy). Duke criteria are applied for diagnostic categorisation. Echocardiography (TTE then TOE if non-diagnostic) is essential and surgical referral is needed for valve perforation, heart failure, persistent bacteraemia, or large vegetations.
key_points: IVDU plus new murmur plus fevers = S. aureus dominant pathogen
Empiric: IV flucloxacillin plus gentamicin
Vancomycin if MRSA risk or penicillin allergy
Three sets of blood cultures before antibiotics
explanation.pearls: Tricuspid valve IE in intravenous drug users frequently presents with septic pulmonary emboli and bilateral cavitating lung infiltrates rather than left-sided heart failure; CT chest should be considered when there is hypoxia even with a normal initial CXR.

stem: A 42-year-old man presents to your emergency department with 5 days of progressive bilateral leg weakness that started in his calves and has ascended to his thighs. He now reports tingling in both hands. He had a brief gastroenteritis illness 2 weeks ago. On examination he has symmetric proximal and distal leg weakness 3/5, areflexia in lower limbs and reduced upper-limb reflexes. He can still walk slowly with assistance. Forced vital capacity is 28 mL/kg. Sensation is intact.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/78, HR 88, RR 18, SpO2 99 percent RA, T 36.8, FVC 28 mL/kg
data_table.Examination: Symmetric flaccid lower limb weakness 3/5; areflexia LL; reduced UL reflexes; no bulbar signs; no autonomic features yet
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal; UEC normal; CK 220; CSF protein 1.4 g/L, WCC 2 x10^6/L; nerve conduction studies show demyelinating pattern; MRI spine pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line disease-modifying treatment?

options.text: High-dose intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days as initial immunomodulation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no proven benefit in Guillain-Barre syndrome; large meta-analyses show no improvement and possible harm. (Source: Cochrane)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily with proton pump inhibitor cover and slow taper over 12 weeks
options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids are not effective in GBS; do not use. (Source: Cochrane)

options.text: Therapeutic plasma exchange as the most cost-effective Australian first-line treatment for this presentation
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is an alternative first-line therapy with similar efficacy to IVIG but is logistically more demanding and less commonly available outside tertiary neurology centres; IVIG is the preferred Australian first-line in most settings. (Source: NBA; BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin 0.4 g/kg daily for 5 consecutive days targeting a total dose of 2 g/kg
options.rationale: IVIG 0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days (total 2 g/kg) is the Australian first-line treatment for GBS within 4 weeks of symptom onset, particularly when patients are non-ambulant or worsening. Plasma exchange is an equivalent alternative when available. (Source: NBA; BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Pyridostigmine 30 mg every 6 hours with regular cardiopulmonary monitoring during titration
options.rationale: Pyridostigmine is for myasthenia gravis; GBS is a neuromuscular junction-independent demyelinating polyneuropathy. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Guillain-Barre syndrome is an acute immune-mediated demyelinating polyneuropathy classically presenting with ascending symmetrical weakness, areflexia, and CSF albuminocytological dissociation (elevated protein with normal cell count). Most cases follow a triggering infection (Campylobacter jejuni is the commonest). FVC less than 20 mL/kg, bulbar weakness, or rapid progression triggers ICU admission. First-line therapies are IVIG 2 g/kg total over 5 days or therapeutic plasma exchange; both are equally efficacious. Corticosteroids are not effective. Treatment is most beneficial within 4 weeks of onset and when patients are unable to walk independently.
key_points: Ascending weakness + areflexia + CSF protein high with normal WCC = GBS
IVIG and plasma exchange are equal first-line
Corticosteroids are NOT effective
Monitor FVC, bulbar function and autonomic status for ICU triggers
explanation.pearls: A 20-30 percent fall in forced vital capacity over 24 hours, or absolute FVC below 20 mL/kg, are objective triggers for ICU admission and elective intubation in Guillain-Barre syndrome; do not wait for desaturation.

subtopic_detail: Intravenous iron when oral fails with time-limited surgical window
stem: A 48-year-old woman presents to your GP clinic with 3 months of fatigue and breathlessness on exertion. She has heavy menstrual bleeding for 18 months, takes ibuprofen frequently for dysmenorrhoea, and has tried oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily for the past 8 weeks. She reports nausea and constipation on the oral iron and intermittent adherence. Her gynaecologist has referred her for endometrial ablation but the procedure is 6 weeks away.

data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae; no koilonychia; cardiovascular and respiratory examinations normal; mild ankle oedema
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L, MCV 70 fL, ferritin 8 mcg/L, transferrin saturation 7 percent, CRP 4; UEC normal; LFTs normal; coeliac serology negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue oral ferrous sulfate for another 12 weeks and recheck full blood count then
options.rationale: Oral iron intolerance and ongoing menstrual loss limit oral repletion success; continuing oral iron is unlikely to achieve correction before scheduled surgery. (Source: BloodSafe; eTG)

options.text: Switch to oral iron polymaltose complex 100 mg daily and review tolerance after 4 weeks
options.rationale: Polymaltose is sometimes better tolerated but still requires months to correct severe iron deficiency anaemia with ongoing losses; not suitable for the 6-week timeframe. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Arrange packed red cell transfusion of 2 units to correct the anaemia rapidly before surgery
options.rationale: Transfusion is reserved for symptomatic severe anaemia (typically Hb below 70 or acute decompensation); not indicated for stable Hb 88 with time for IV iron correction. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management)

options.text: Commence erythropoietin-stimulating agent subcutaneously 4-weekly until ferritin normalises
options.rationale: ESAs are not first-line in iron deficiency anaemia; they require adequate iron stores to be effective and are reserved for chronic kidney disease and chemotherapy-related anaemia. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Arrange intravenous ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg infusion at the general practice infusion clinic
options.rationale: Ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg single infusion is appropriate when oral iron is ineffective, not tolerated, or where rapid repletion is required (within 8 weeks); reticulocyte response begins within 5-7 days and Hb rises 20-30 g/L by 4 weeks. (Source: AMH; NBA; BloodSafe)

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency anaemia from ongoing menstrual loss with oral iron failure and a time-limited surgical window is a clear indication for parenteral iron in Australia. Ferric carboxymaltose is the preferred IV iron preparation (single infusion up to 1000 mg over 15 minutes, repeat at 1 week if larger replacement needed). Common adverse effects include hypophosphataemia, infusion-site reactions, and transient skin pigmentation. PBS-subsidised for documented iron deficiency where oral iron has failed or is contraindicated. Address ongoing source (menorrhagia management) in parallel.
key_points: IV iron when oral fails, not tolerated, or rapid repletion needed
Ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg as single infusion is Australian preferred
Treat the source: tranexamic acid, hormonal management, surgical option
Recheck Hb and ferritin 4-8 weeks after infusion
explanation.pearls: Severe symptomatic hypophosphataemia (phosphate below 0.32 mmol/L) occurs in around 1-3 percent of patients receiving ferric carboxymaltose with bone pain, fatigue and proximal weakness, typically peaking at 2 weeks - check phosphate if symptoms appear at follow-up.
